text:
*[Bug 19071] wilcox.test and precision when detecting zeros*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19071>

--- Comment #7 from Kurt Hornik (<mailto:Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at|Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at>) ---
I guess we all agree that the test should be scale invariant.

Now, if we so a signed rank test where all inputs are of the order
1e-18, the test cannot know whether these are all rounding errors or
from measurements done on a exa scale :-)

I would not mind reusing a given digits.rank for determining zeros via
zapsmall: if this is inappropriate, users could simply not give
digits.rank and transform the inputs themselves as appropriate?

elements.text: [Bug 19071] wilcox.test and precision when detecting zeros

text:
*[Bug 19069] aperm(X, 1:length(dim(X))) should short-circuit and just return X.*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19069>

--- Comment #7 from Kurt Hornik (<mailto:Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at|Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at>) ---
Very confusing ...

Apparently, t() by default preserves classes.  The docs say the value is

     A matrix, with ‘dim’ and ‘dimnames’ constructed appropriately from
     those of ‘x’, and other attributes except names copied across.

which might include the class attribute: the C code indeed has

    copyMostAttrib(a, r);

For aperm(), we say

     A transposed version of array ‘a’, with subscripts permuted as
     indicated by the array ‘perm’.  If ‘resize’ is ‘TRUE’, the array
     is reshaped as well as having its elements permuted, the
     ‘dimnames’ are also permuted; if ‘resize = FALSE’ then the
     returned object has the same dimensions as ‘a’, and the dimnames
     are dropped.  In each case other attributes are copied from ‘a’.

but that clearly is not happening in the C code.

The above indeed promises to drop the dimnames if resize = FALSE, even
when not permuting.  Strange.

I guess we need to first decide about the

  and other attributes except names copied across.

contract.

elements.text: [Bug 19069] aperm(X, 1:length(dim(X))) should short-circuit and just return X.

text:
*[Bug 19069] aperm(X, 1:length(dim(X))) should short-circuit and just return X.*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19069>

--- Comment #8 from Kurt Hornik (<mailto:Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at|Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at>) ---
Btw, afaict even in very old versions of R we never copied attributes.
c38940 changed

-  \code{dimnames} are also permuted;
-  if \code{FALSE} then the returned object has the same dimensions as
-  \code{a}, and the dimnames are dropped.
+  \code{dimnames} are also permuted; if \code{resize = FALSE} then the
+  returned object has the same dimensions as \code{a}, and the dimnames
+  are dropped.  In each case other attributes are copied from \code{a}.

presumably copying the change from t.Rd.

+  are dropped.  In each case other attributes are copied from \code{a}.

presumably copying the change from t.Rd.

text:
*[Bug 19069] aperm(X, 1:length(dim(X))) should short-circuit and just return X.*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19069>

--- Comment #9 from Pavel N. Krivitsky (<mailto:p.krivitsky@unsw.edu.au|p.krivitsky@unsw.edu.au>) ---
In my opinion, it would be better to bring the implementation into agreement
with the documentation, as opposed to the other way around, and to ask the
package maintainers to insert an `unclass()` in their `apply()` calls if they
do something unusual with the `[` method. My reasons:

1. Relying on undocumented behaviour is one thing; relying on behaviour
contrary to documentation is another.
2. It's consistent with `t()`.
3. It's more useful for a developer: the code to restore attributes after
removal requires saving the starting attributes, whereas removing attributes
does not.

Though, now that I look, I don't think I quite understand what `resize` is
actually supposed to control. Does it determine how to handle a situation in
which `length(a) != prod(dim(a))`? But, `array()` docs say "the product of the
values must match the length of the array."

--- Comment #10 from Suharto Anggono (<mailto:suharto_anggono@yahoo.com|suharto_anggono@yahoo.com>) ---
(In reply to Pavel N. Krivitsky from comment #9)
`resize = FALSE` plugs the _original_ `dim` to the permuted array.

text:
*[Bug 18743] "hclust()" documentation about "ward.D" and "ward.D2" need to be revised. In addition to the incorrect description of the "ward.D" and "ward.D2" options, there is a lack of correct information about the value of d to be set.*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18743>

--- Comment #10 from Atsushi Kimura (<mailto:atkimura@nstac.go.jp|atkimura@nstac.go.jp>) ---
I translated the "Introduction" section of 202204_ESTRELA_22-27_StudyNote.pdf
into English.

====================================
1. Introduction
In the March 2021 issue of this journal, we published an article on
hierarchical cluster analysis of household survey data (Kimura and
Takabe [1]).
Several readers of the article commented that they were interested in
cluster analysis but didn't understand the differences in options for
the Ward method in R's hclust function, how to use it correctly, or how
to interpret the analysis results.
Osumi [2] points out that, as is common in hierarchical classification,
even with the same method name, differences in the selection of
similarity/dissimilarity, the stratification procedure, and the use of
independently defined options can result in completely different output
results for the same data depending on the software used.
Murtagh and Legendre [3] also indicate that there are multiple
variations of Ward method statistical software available and warn
against their use. While the R hclust function subsequently received
additional features, including the ward.D and ward.D2 options, the R
documentation is not particularly helpful for beginners. Inaccurate
descriptions can be found in information available on the internet and
elsewhere. It is understandable that users may be confused.

This article explains the differences between the ward.D and ward.D2
options of the hclust function currently in use since version 3.0.3,
their correct usage, and the basic concepts and characteristics of
Ward[4]. Furthermore, it compares the results of applying a program
implemented independently by the author based on Ward[4] with the hclust
function to an actual data analysis example[1], verifying that the
internal processing of the hclust function is consistent with what has
been described. The design and implementation of the independently
implemented program used here will be explained in detail in the next
article. This should serve as a useful reference for concrete examples
of implementing mathematical formulas and algorithms in a program. The
views expressed in this article are those of the author only and do not
represent those of the organization to which the author belongs.
Furthermore, any errors or omissions in this text are solely the
responsibility of the author.
==================

) ---
